data.title: 4 Signs Of Increasing Consumer Stress
data.description:
The U.S. markets have remained quite resilient this week despite the escalating conflict in the Middle East and the associated spike in oil prices. The narrative around the AI revolution remains intact and serves as the key driver of GDP growth in the United States.

Consumer spending is holding up despite lousy consumer sentiment readings, although retail sales were flat in December and down 0.2% month-over-month in January. This is largely due to the bifurcated U.S. economy, which has become known as this K-shaped economy. The top 10% of American households by income now account for roughly half of all U.S. consumer spending. As long as the stock market and real estate markets continue to hold up, this cohort should continue to spend confidently.

However, most of the rest of the consumer base is struggling. And while the AI revolution has dominated the economic and equity headlines in recent years, the U.S. consumer still drives roughly two thirds of GDP in the country. In today's column, I will highlight four concerning signs that the majority of consumers are seeing ever increasing stress.

#1 - Personal Savings Rate

Following the onset of Covid and the pandemic lockdowns, Congress pumped trillions of dollars of additional largesse into the U.S. economy via legislation. A decent chunk of this was via Covid stimulus payments. This bolstered the personal savings rate in the U.S. by a huge measure, as can be seen below.

However, these funds have now been depleted, and the U.S. savings rate stands at a minuscule 3.6%. Higher tax tax refunds this year and recent measures like no tax on the first $25,000 annually of tip income should help over the next couple of quarters, but only marginally.

#2 - Consumer Loan Delinquencies & Sign Of Desperation

Consumer loan delinquencies are rising on student, auto, and credit card debt, as can be seen above. Combined, this represents approximately $4.5 trillion in overall debt. You can see signs of increasing desperation to service consumer debt. A record percentage of Americans are raiding their 401(k) accounts for funds, according to the latest read from Vanguard. This is unfortunately a continuation of a trend that has developed in recent years as inflation has run much hotter than in previous decades.

#3 - Energy Prices Spiking

This is the latest headwind consumers now face. Rising energy costs as the result of the escalating conflict in the Middle East. Already, the average price of a gallon of gasoline has jumped to $3.32 per gallon, its highest level in 18 months. Diesel is over $4.25 a gallon, a level last seen in November 2023. Jet fuel prices have gone up 15% since the bombing over Iran began.

Things could get much worse if most of the oil exports from the Middle East remain locked in. Qatar’s energy minister, as reported by the Financial Times, warned that the conflict may force Gulf energy exporters to shut down production within weeks. Other Gulf states might follow suit.

#4 - Accelerating Jobs Markets Deterioration

With recent revisions, 2025 showed job growth of roughly 15,000 positions a month. A dramatic downturn from recent years following the worst of the Covid pandemic. On Friday, the February BLS jobs report showed a print of a negative 92,000. This was a brutal miss compared to the consensus that was calling for an increase of 55,000. The two prior months were revised down by a total of 86,000 jobs as well. The unemployment rate moved to 4.4% from 4.3% previously.

Despite the huge jobs miss, the yield on the 10-year Treasury did not fall as it usually does in that scenario as oil prices continued to spike on Friday. How much of an impact AI is having on job growth is still speculation at this point, but likely to get more negative in the months ahead. Block (XYZ) announced it was laying off 40% of its entire staff, as those functions will be replaced by AI. Oracle (ORCL) just disclosed new reductions in force to free up investment for its massive AI capex. Overall, information technology lost 11,000 positions in February.

In summary, the surge of tech spending around the buildout of AI infrastructure will continue to be the key economic engine for the U.S. economy, at least through 2026, given capex budgets are committed to at the big hyperscalers.

However, if things continue to deteriorate on the consumer front, will that be enough to deliver decent economic growth in 2026? Will stock market valuations hold if GDP growth projections get ratcheted down in the months ahead?

I have my doubts, as with job growth clearly slowing and now negative so far in 2026, consumer stress continuing to rise, and new inflationary pressures from surging energy prices, the most likely economic scenario ahead feels like stagflation. And that is not an environment equities perform well in historically. And investors should remember the bear markets that occurred due to a rapid surge in oil prices triggered by unexpected events in the Middle East, like in 1973, 1979, and 1990.

data.title: Nvidia: Blowout Q4 Report Aside, Don't Underestimate The Power Of CUDA
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my last article on Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), published in November 2025, I analyzed the company’s Q3 report and explored the key takeaways, with a special focus on the rising competition from the likes of Alphabet (GOOGL) and on a potential partnership between (META) and GOOGL. I had a Strong Buy rating on the stock.

Since my article was published, the stock has gained 1.71%, outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500), which gained 0.27% during the same period.

NVDA reported its Q4 numbers about two weeks ago and despite a stellar report, after an initial pop during the after-hours, the stock gave all its gains and then some during regular trading hours. This has become a familiar pattern in recent times, which is unfortunate given that the company continues to beat no matter how high the bar is.

In this article, I analyze the company’s Q4 report and explore the key takeaways. I focus predominantly on the surge in supply commitments that was announced by the company and what it means for investors, as well as analyze just how intense the competitive environment has become for NVDA and whether investors have reasons to worry.

A Snapshot of Nvidia’s Q4 Performance

NVDA finished the year with a blockbuster quarterly performance, beating on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. More specifically, Q4 revenues came in at $68.1 billion, up 73% y/y and 20% q/q, comfortably beating analyst estimates by $1.90 billion. Non-GAAP diluted EPS came in at $1.62, up 82% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $0.08. Data center revenues set a quarterly record, coming in at $62.3 billion, up 75% y/y and 22% q/q, blowing past estimates of $60.4 billion. Q4 gross margins, on a non-GAAP basis, came in at 75.2%, expanding by 160 bps (or 1.6%) sequentially and were in line with analyst estimates. Operation cash flows came in at $36.2 billion, which translates to a y/y growth of 117.8%.

Q1 FY27 guidance also came in very strong. More specifically, Q1 revenues are projected to come in at $78 billion [+/- 2%], which blew past consensus estimates of $72 billion. Non-GAAP gross margins are expected to come in at 75% [+/- 0.5%]. Non-GAAP operating expenses are projected to be approximately $7.5 billion [includes $1.9 billion of stock-based compensation). Finally, non-GAAP tax rates are expected to be in the range between 17% and 19%.

NVDA’s Supply Commitment Surge Lends Credibility to Company’s Growth Story

One of the highlights of NVDA’s Q4 earnings call was when CEO Jensen Huang declared that “the agentic AI inflection point has arrived.” While it’s too early to say whether this point has arrived or not, one thing that is certain is the progress made on agentic AI by the likes of (OPENAI), Anthropic (ANTHRO) and the hyperscalers has only fueled NVDA’s growth story. This can be seen in both the exponential growth in data center revenues in Q4 as well as the increased visibility of strong, sustained demand for its GPUs in FY27.

For instance, as mentioned earlier, data center revenues set a record in Q4 at $62.3 billion. It was the second straight quarter of 20%+ growth and blew past not just analyst estimates, but also the so-called "whisper number."

However, the biggest takeaway for me from NVDA’s Q4 report was that we got further confirmation that the $500 billion in orders throughout 2026, which management had announced a while back, will be exceeded, a point I had touched upon in my previous article, and one that was confirmed by CFO Collette Kress during the Q4 earnings call. More importantly, she also confirmed that the company has both the inventory and supply commitments in place to meet this $500+ billion demand, “including shipments extending into calendar 2027.”This was further confirmed quantitatively when the company announced that the total supply-related commitments hit $95.2 billion by end of Q4 from $50.3 billion at the end of Q3, which lends further proof that the company has the capacity to meet the demand “beyond the next several quarters.”

Overall, the key takeaway that investors should pay more attention to, in my opinion, from NVDA’s Q4 report, is the confirmation of not only having an explosive demand but also the company’s ability to meet the demand in FY27. Having merely demand means nothing in my view. It’s all about how soon the demand can be met. The comment from the CFO, therefore, on the confirmed visibility of meeting the demand, at least in the next twelve months, should be reassuring for investors.

NVDA’s Market Leadership Unlikely To be Threatened Even as Competitors Circle Around

In my last article on NVDA, I argued about how the reaction to a potential META-GOOGL partnership being a serious threat to NVDA was overdone. Q4 offered further confirmation of this. More specifically, NVDA announced that META plans to deploy “millions of Blackwells and Rubin GPUs, NVIDIA CPUs and Spectrum x Ethernet for training and inference.”

Investors should take note that the company has only just shipped its first Vera Rubin samples to customers two weeks back, and the production of these will not commence until the second half of the year. Therefore, for a company like META to already express concrete plans to deploy them for training their AI models is further evidence that NVDA’s market leadership is unlikely to be threatened by the likes of GOOGL or AMD anytime soon. This is because, in my opinion, even if competitors’ products come close from a performance perspective, no competitor has come close to NVDA’s software stack, CUDA.

Take the case of Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), for instance. The company’s Helios [MI455X] offers 432 GB capacity per GPU compared to NVDA’s Very Rubin [R200]’s 288 GB capacity per GPU. Furthermore, on paper, the Helios is projected to have 2.9 exaFLOPS of FP4 reference at rack scale, which makes it faster relative to the R200. Finally, AMD utilizes TSMC's (TSM) 2nm/3nm hybrid chiplet design, making Helios the first GPU to take advantage of the N2 node. As such, there are quite a few advantages that AMD’s Helios has over Vera Rubin, which could make it attractive.

However, when it comes to software stack, AMD is unlikely to replicate the performance of CUDA with its Radeon Open Compute [ROCm] software ecosystem anytime soon. Therein lies the challenge, in my opinion, making it harder for competitors like AMD to displace NVDA’s market leadership. For instance, while the ROCm has made substantial progress in recent months, successfully narrowing the performance gap with CUDA from 40-50% to approximately 10-30%, several challenges still remain, the most important being CUDA’s nearly two-decade lead in optimization and stability.

Moreover, performance-critical GPU workloads remain deeply entrenched in CUDA-specific optimization and while the introduction of AI agents (e.g., Claude Code) has made porting from CUDA to ROCm faster, the ROCm installation still needs more technical expertise (e.g., Linux kernel and driver configuration tweaks) compared to the plug-and-play nature of CUDA.

Finally, when it comes to real-world workload, the predecessor of the MI455X, the MI300X, underperformed the GB200, as NVDA’s throughput advantage increased from 77% at light workloads to more than 105% at high concurrency. This was attributed to the ability of CUDA’s more mature execution stack to continue scaling as workload intensity increased, with the ROCm plateauing earlier. There is a possibility that the MI455X may narrow the gap thanks to its architectural improvements. However, given that CUDA’s advantage is structural and not hardware-dependent, the real-world workload issue for ROCm is unlikely to be resolved by a chip upgrade alone.

The list of CUDA’s advantages doesn’t end there, but it does paint a bigger picture, which is that for all the progress made by the competition when it comes to chip alternatives, when it comes to the software ecosystem, NVDA’s market dominance remains intact. This, in my view, should keep NVDA at the forefront of the AI revolution for the foreseeable future.

Valuation

Forward P/E Multiple Approach

Price Target

$273.00

Projected Forward P/E multiple

32x

Projected EPS in FY28

$8.53

Source: Author’s xGBoost Valuation Model.

To calculate the EPS for FY28 [CY 2027], I deployed an ensemble model, which combines a weighted log-linear trend model and a machine learning model [direct 8-horizon XGBoost]. This ensemble model is an upgraded version of the ensemble model that I used to value NVDA in my last article.

The two components can be described as follows:

  • Weighted Log-Linear Trend Model – this model replaces the regime-switching dynamic growth model and helps to extrapolate NVDA’s current growth trajectory rather than decaying it towards a historical average that has become stale due to the ongoing “AI infrastructure regime”, which began back in Q3 2022.
  • Direct 8-Horizon XGBoost Machine Learning Model – this model overcomes the compounding forecast error associated with the previously used recursive random forest ML model. To eliminate the compounding forecast error, a separate model is trained for each of the eight forecast quarters independently. The machine learning model was trained on a panel dataset covering seven semiconductor companies [NVDA, AMD, Broadcom (AVGO), Qualcomm (QCOM), Micron (MU), Marvell (MRVL), TSM], which allowed it to learn the hyper-growth patterns at scale across the broader peer universe.

I undertook a walk-forward validation test for both the models, based on the most recent fourth quarters. The model metrics for each of the models are as follow:

Metric

Log-Linear Trend

XGBoost

Notes

Mean Absolute Error (MAE)

$0.27

$0.02

Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)

36.37%

3.08%

A MAPE less than 5% is considered to be excellent.

A MAPE of 3.08% for the XGBoost implies that it was better suited to determine NVDA’s valuation compared to the recursive random forest that I used in my previous article, which had a MAPE of 10.64%.

The extreme differential [11.8x] in the performance gap between the log-linear trend and the XGBoost models triggered my ensemble model’s dominance gate. The dominance gate is a safeguard that gets triggered when there is an extreme differential and operates as follows: if one model’s MAPE exceeds 2.5x the other, the weaker model is dropped entirely to avoid the addition of noise. Therefore, given the 11.8x differential, the dominance gate was triggered as a result of which, the EPS forecasting relied entirely on the XGBoost model.

The projected EPS for FY28, as predicted by the XGBoost model is as follows:

Total FY28 EPS

$8.53

Quarter-End

Projected FY28 EPS

Q1 FY28

$1.89

Q2 FY28

$2.12

Q3 FY28

$2.00

Q4 FY28

$2.52

The price target was calculated based on the forward P/E method under three different scenarios (Bear Case, Base Case, and Bull Case). Each of the scenarios, along with the assigned forward P/E are described below:

Scenario

Description

Forward P/E

Current Forward P/E = 22.2x (Source: Seeking Alpha)

Bear

NVDA’s Q4 supply commitments prove to be front-loaded; competitors grab more market share at a faster pace; Rubin ramp compresses gross margins below 72%

22x

Base

Reflects the Q4 FY2026 reality, Supply commitments confirm capacity to meet CY2026 & CY2027order book; Gross margins remain at the mid-70% mark; CUDA ecosystem maintains lead

35x

Bull

Agentic AI inflection point materializes; Order book significantly exceeds company’s estimates; Competitive intensity falls short of projections allowing NVDA to maintain/expand its market lead; Vera Rubin and next generations’ demand accelerate; NVDA becomes de-facto AI infrastructure monopoly

45x

The price target under each of the scenarios are given below

Scenario

Forward P/E

Projected FY28 EPS

Price Target (forward P/E x Projected FY28 EPS)

Bear

22x

$8.53

$188

Base

32x

$8.53

$273

Bull

45x

$8.53

$384

The price target under the Base case scenario suggests an upside of about 50% from current levels. Given that even the price target under the bear case scenario shows a marginal upside from current levels, I am maintaining my Strong Buy rating on the stock.

The negativity surrounding the stock that I mentioned in my last article continues to persist, as evidenced by both a weaker momentum grade [B] on Seeking Alpha’s momentum scale and the disappointing post earnings reaction despite a stellar report. As such, I would recommend a dollar cost-averaging strategy while buying this semiconductor giant.

Risk Factors

In addition to the factors mentioned in my bear case scenario as well as the key risk that I mentioned in my previous article surrounding the potential implosion of the “circular deals,” the other risk factor to my investment thesis remains the potential of the likes of AMD to close the gap on NVDA when it comes to the software ecosystem. AMD, for instance, has made leaps and bounds with respect to Helios, as I mentioned earlier. Should the acceleration persist, then the dominance of CUDA would erode, which would subsequently have an adverse effect on the ability of NVDA to maintain its market leadership.

Concluding Thoughts

It was yet another blowout quarter from NVDA, with the company beating every key measurable street estimate. The data center revenues resumed their accelerating growth and the company confirmed that it would exceed its $500 billion sales estimate and even announced a massive supply commitment to meet the demand to support the confirmation.

There has been a lot of talk recently about how the competitive landscape has become more treacherous for NVDA. While it is true that the number of competitors has increased and NVDA’s market share certainly has gone down, investors often overlook the fact that it’s not only the chips that matter but also the software ecosystem. In that respect, CUDA continues to dominate and while the likes of AMD have certainly closed the gap, the head start that CUDA has had continues to ensure that it’s leadership remains intact. And with more efficient GPUs set to arrive in the form of Vera Rubin, the combination of these and CUDA are likely to ensure that NVDA’s market dominance remains intact for the foreseeable future, as agentic AI reaches an inflection point.

From a valuation perspective, based on the results of the XGBoost machine learning model that I deployed, I see considerable upside at current levels. Therefore, I maintain a Strong Buy rating on the stock and recommend a dollar cost averaging strategy given the relative weak momentum as well as the continued uncertainty surrounding the ongoing geopolitical conflict, which is driving down investor sentiment. There is no debating the fact that competitive intensity has increased for NVDA when it comes to chips. CUDA, however, shields NVDA’s market dominance from this intensity.

data.title: Sensient: Traders Overreacted To The Natural Colorings Thesis
data.description:
I last covered ingredients company Sensient Technologies Corporation (NYSE:SXT) in early 2025. At the time, I credited the firm for its improving operating results but said that it was too early to buy. That initially looked like a poor call, as SXT proceeded to rally sharply later that summer. However, Sensient has now dipped 20% off the 52-week highs. And, in the bigger picture, after 2025's failed breakout, Sensient shares have slid back to around where they were trading five years ago:

So, is it time to buy SXT stock? Here’s why I’m still not convinced that Sensient is a good opportunity just yet.

The Upside Case: Natural Colorings Will Take Off

It's no secret that Dept. of Health and Human Services "HHS" Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has focused on changing America's food landscape. That is part of his "Make America Healthy Again" program. It'd be logical to assume that there would be some federal policy pushing food companies to use healthier or more natural ingredients as a result of RFK. Jr.'s persuasion.

However, interestingly, it appears to be state efforts that are more directly causing action, at least so far. West Virginia, for example, became the first state to ban the sales of foods with synthetic colors, though the law is currently being contested in court. For another example, Texas passed legislation that will force food makers to put warning labels on products that contain certain ingredients, including synthetic colors and titanium dioxide. That law is slated to go into effect in 2027.

While most states have not passed any legislation on these matters, the fact that a couple of states have done so is causing many packaged food companies to change their ingredients regardless. It's inconvenient and more expensive to have to manufacture a certain cereal for 48 states and then have a different set of ingredients for West Virginia and a separate packaging label for Texas. Easier, in many cases, to simply remove the offending ingredient entirely for the whole nation in order to streamline product manufacturing and distribution.

In addition, some private companies are starting to shift their product composition in order to get ahead of these rules. Retailing giant Walmart (WMT) announced that it will eliminate synthetic dyes from all of its private label goods within the next year.

As per Sentient's management, there is about ten times the revenue opportunity for the company in selling coloring made from natural ingredients instead of synthetics. Thus, in theory, this could lead to a windfall for Sensient's natural colors business and lead to a major growth engine for a firm that has otherwise struggled to increase organic sales over the past decade. And to that point, Sensient is investing significantly in new capacity to build out its natural colors business.

A Modest Impact To Operating Results So Far

Sensient stock blasted off in early 2025 as analysts noted that Sensient could become a big winner from the Make America Healthy Again movement. And, given the analysis I presented above, this is a reasonable enough bullish thesis.

However, we're now almost a year removed from the initial excitement around natural colorings. But it hasn't really made any real impact on Sensient's results yet.

Take this quote from the company’s Q4 conference call, for example:

“While some tariff-induced customer supply chain disruptions and unforeseen weather events negatively impacted our fourth quarter results, we still reported 2% local currency revenue growth and flat adjusted local currency operating profit in the fourth quarter.”

I don’t see 2% revenue growth and flat operating profits as something that’s worth SXT stock's 25x forward earnings multiple in the current operating environment.

Investors can reasonably argue that growth is going to accelerate as packaged food companies search for more natural coloring solutions. Perhaps that’s the case. However, the U.S. alone makes up just 5% of the world’s population; more stringent regulations in that market by itself aren’t necessarily a complete game-changer for Sensient. If many other international markets copy the tougher regulations that we're seeing in states like Texas and West Virginia, that would drive a large fundamental shift in the market. But that remains to be seen. It’s also worth remembering that RFK. Jr. is likely to remain in charge of the HHS only through the end of President Trump’s term, meaning that much of the current momentum around natural colorings could subside or even start to reverse in 2029.

Meanwhile, Sensient has its agricultural ingredients business as well, and sales were down outright there in Q4. The company blamed weather issues, such as historic rains, for the setbacks. Regardless, I don’t see products such as dehydrated onions and garlic as ever being especially high-margin or significant growth avenues.

Sensient Stock Verdict

I’m not against the idea of owning Sensient or another flavorings and colorings sort of company. That said, I don’t believe this sort of business is worth an especially high multiple. I see folks compare ingredient companies like Sensient or International Flavors & Fragrances (IFF) to, for example, McCormick (MKC).

However, I think that’s an unfair comparison. McCormick has branded consumer products such as Frank’s RedHot, Cholula, and Old Bay, which have strong loyalty and some degree of pricing power. You could argue that a spice like black pepper or oregano is a commodity in the same way that food colorings are, and that’s probably true to an extent. But I believe that a company that gets much of its business from end consumers can defend higher profit margins and thus a higher valuation multiple than a company like Sensient, which primarily sells to packaged food companies.

In my mind, it simply doesn’t make much sense to pay 25x forward earnings for Sensient, especially in a world where a company like McCormick, which usually trades at a premium valuation, is instead being offered at 21x. To that point, I believe potential Sensient buyers must take into account the fact that the valuations of packaged food companies and consumer staples companies more broadly have taken hits. There’s likely to be less demand for a premium-valued name like Sensient at a time when so many of its peers are selling near 5-to-10-year valuation lows.

With this in mind, it seems rather generous for investors to be assigning a 25x forward P/E multiple to Sensient for potential growth in one of its divisions when even that story really hasn’t played out all that much in the underlying operating results.

To be fair, analysts see Sensient's top-line growth accelerating to 8% in 2026 and 9% in 2027. If that happens, I think shares are reasonably priced at today’s levels. Thus, I rate the stock as a hold today.

But it's worth remembering that Sensient generated $1.5 billion of revenues way back in 2014. It did $1.6 billion last year. That's effectively no growth for more than a decade, certainly not enough to keep up with inflation. For the stock to be reasonably valued at this point, we have to assume that this coloring growth opportunity will transform this stodgy enterprise into a nearly 10% per year growth company going forward. That could well happen, but with the market already pricing that in as the base case, there's not enough potential upside to warrant a buy.

data.title: Potential Tender Offer At MFS High Income Municipal Trust Before Vote
data.description:
I expect to see a tender offer plan announced for MFS High Income Municipal Trust (CXE) in the coming days or weeks. This is among three municipal Closed End Funds (“CEFs”) heavily owned by well-recognized institutional investors that have been subject to shareholder votes needed to approve both a merger and a new advisor.

Earlier this week, the first two (CMU and CXH) announced the boards had approved 50% tender offers at 99% of NAV contingent on shareholder approval. Rationale in each case pointed to encouraging support for the reorganization and providing shareholders with liquidity in advance.

CXE has been the most heavily market discounted of the three funds with high institutional ownership. Its largest institutional shareholders include Saba Capital Management, Almitas Capital, and RiverNorth. In this case, I anticipate all three and other institutional shareholders will ultimately vote for the CXE reorganization if a similar liquidity event exists for CXE.

Background Information

CXE trades around 92 cents per dollar of NAV and pays a municipal income yield of 5.68%. The fund is diversified across states and has been a category underperformer for the last year (3.6% vs 7.3% category NAV) and 10 years (2.25% vs 2.69% category NAV). To date, CXE's bond portfolio has been managed by a well-known US Investment Advisor, MFS Investment Management. MFS was founded in 1924 by George Putnam and is famous for launching the first mutual fund designed for everyday investors, now known as Massachusetts Investors Trust.

CXE has been subject to shareholder Activism by Saba in recent years. Saba filed a 13-D in July of 2023 and has since filed numerous amendments. CXE is also a holding in Saba’s exchange-traded fund (CEFS), which is primarily owned by retail investors.

Last December, MFS and Aberdeen announced planned reorganizations whereby MFS chose to exit its CEF Advisory business in favor of a UK-based advisor named Aberdeen. CMU, CXE, and CXH would be merged into MFS Municipal Income Trust (MFM) with Aberdeen becoming the new investment advisor.

What Are The Risks?

CXE risks merging into MFM, which currently trades at an 8.1% discount to NAV absent any preceding liquidity options if the Board pursues and achieves the vote without the tender offer, my analysis suggests. The eventual distribution rate of MFM has not been finalized. Fund mergers can offer better scale. Still, there would likely be meaningful selling pressure in MFM from those prior shareholders of CXE who did not support the reorganization nor have an opportunity to exit near NAV. To be clear, an excess supply from shareholders who want an exit looks like a far bigger potential problem than any benefits from scale would offset. I expect to lose money on my CXE investment if a tender offer is not announced.

Still, the bigger risk appears to be selling at market now in advance of knowing what liquidity options will exist.

Most Likely Outcome

This looks like a buy because I do not expect the merger and new advisor votes will pass without a tender offer for CXE. The vote typically needed per the Investment Company Act of 1940 amounts to the lesser of 67% or more of shares present at a meeting (if over 50% of outstanding shares are present) or more than 50% of total outstanding shares.

data.title: Munich RE: Income Remains The Most Appealing Factor Of Its Investment Case Following 2025 Earnings
data.description:
As I've covered in the past, Münchener Rückversicherungs-Gesellschaft Aktiengesellschaft in München (MURGY), aka Munich RE, offers a sustainable dividend and has good business fundamentals, but I saw upside potential as limited due to its premium valuation at the time. Not surprisingly, its shares have traded sideways over the past few months and have underperformed slightly the overall stock market (SPY) since my last article, as shown in the next graph.

Despite this relatively weak share price performance, Munich RE’s operating momentum can be considered positive in recent quarters, as the company was able to report higher earnings during 2025 despite some top-line softness.

Indeed, following some strong years for the reinsurance industry, especially in the Property & Casualty (P&C) segment, pricing was softer over the last year as the pricing peak seems to have been reached in the current cycle. As can be seen in the next graph, price renewals were negative during last year, contrary to previous years, and this trend continued regarding January 2026 renewals. Given that most renewals happen at the beginning of the year, this does not bode well for top-line performance this year.

Moreover, on top of negative pricing on reinsurance policies, volumes were also weak during 2025, declining by 1.5% YoY. Regarding January 2026 renewals, volume decreased by 7.8% YoY, as the company was selective in its underwriting criteria and decided to reduce risk where it didn’t consider it was reaching its return requirements. Additionally, competition has focused mainly on pricing in recent renewals, putting pressure on the profitability of the sector, which is a good signal that Munich RE decided to walk away from some risks, as the company seems to not be seeking volumes at the expense of future losses ahead.

This is important because to maintain sound underwriting results over the long term in the insurance industry, companies need to maintain their underwriting criteria and not react to cyclical changes in the market, something that sometimes is "easier said than done." In the short term, this leads to lower volumes and some top-line pressure, but over the medium-to-long term, it is critical for insurance companies to maintain combined ratios at healthy levels. This happens because the insurance industry is cyclical, and periods of relatively low claims costs are usually followed by years of more events and higher costs.

Therefore, I don’t see Munich RE’s volume weakness as concerning, as the company’s business strategy is to be conservative and be profitable throughout the cycle. Following some ‘hard’ years, Munich RE is now in a good position to be nimble about the risks it’s willing to take, putting profitability as a main goal instead of business growth, a strategy that seems to be prudent over the long haul.

Taking this backdrop into account, Munich RE’s insurance revenue amounted to €60.4 billion in 2025, representing a decline of 0.7% YoY. This was largely caused by weakness in the P&C segment, which is the company’s largest segment, reporting a decline in insurance revenues of 8% compared to the previous year. On the other hand, its primary insurance international operations (ERGO International) were able to report insurance revenue growth of 10% during last year, supported both by organic initiatives and acquisitions, and life reinsurance also increased revenues in 2025, being important contributors to offset weakness in P&C reinsurance.

Regarding investment results, Munich RE has been able to reinvest securities at higher yields over the last few years, leading to increasing recurring income from its investment portfolio. This was the main driver of higher investment results during 2025, which amounted to more than €7.5 billion (+4.5% YoY), being important to offset weaker revenue trends from the insurance segment.

Its investment portfolio yield is now around 3.7%, without considering asset sales or write-downs, which is above current market rates in Europe. This means further increases in the company’s investment returns may be harder to achieve ahead. Thus, I expect its top line to be relatively stable, or down, during 2026, as both investment returns and insurance premiums aren’t likely to increase over the coming quarters.

Indeed, in the P&C reinsurance segment, January renewals are very important and usually set pricing for the rest of the year, given that catastrophe events tend to be more frequent in the second half of the year, for example, related to the hurricane season in North America. Therefore, April and July renewals don’t change much compared to January renewals regarding pricing, leading to a relatively weak outlook for reinsurance revenues during 2026.

Despite some top-line weakness, claims costs were soft during last year, as catastrophe events were not very damaging, leading to a decline of 4% YoY on claims costs. This was the main driver behind earnings growth, with its net profit increasing by 7.6% YoY to €6.1 billion in 2025, being slightly above the company’s own guidance. Its return on equity (ROE) ratio was 18.3% over the last year, up by 10 basis points compared to 2024, remaining at a very good level of profitability compared to its peers and largely above its own cost of capital.

Regarding its capital levels, Munich RE has maintained a very strong position, measured by its Solvency II ratio of 298% at the end of last year. This ratio is way above its internal target range of being above 200% by 2030, which means the company has an excess capital position and can return significant capital to shareholders, even if earnings growth slows down over the next few years.

Given this background, Munich RE has been much more aggressive in recent years in returning capital to shareholders, both through dividends and share buybacks. Its total payout ratio has been relatively high over the past five years, as the company clearly does not need to retain many earnings due to its strong capitalization.

While insurance companies are usually conservative and target capital returns between 50% and 70% of annual earnings, Munich RE has set its total payout ratio between 76% and 87% in recent years, a trend that is likely to be maintained ahead.

Related to 2025 earnings, its dividend was set at €24 per share, being above market expectations and my own estimate of about €23 per share when I last covered it a few months ago. At its current share price, this leads to a dividend yield of about 4.5%, based on its main listing in Germany, which is attractive for income-oriented investors.

Its annual €24 dividend per share represents annual growth of nearly 20%, being much higher than its target of delivering annual dividend growth above 5%. This shows that Munich RE has a very comfortable financial position and is willing to return more capital to shareholders than it was expecting, being a good signal for dividend growth ahead.

Given that its target is to report annual EPS growth above 8% from 2025 to 2030, I think Munich RE’s dividend growth is likely to be at double digits during the next few years. Its target is to maintain total payout returns above 80% of annual earnings during this period, which means Munich RE should continue to deliver a growing dividend and perform share buybacks ahead, being an important support for its share price.

However, this seems to be largely priced in, as Munich RE is currently trading at about 2.05x book value, which is close to the top of its historical range over the last decade, as shown in the next graph. Compared to its closest peers, Hannover RE (HVRRY) and Swiss RE (SSREY), this valuation seems fair, as higher valuation multiples have been a constant trend across the industry.

On the other hand, from an income perspective, Munich RE is offering a slightly higher dividend yield than Swiss RE and much higher than Hannover RE, making it a good play from an income perspective in the European reinsurance sector.

Conclusion

Munich RE was able to report a positive operating performance during last year, even though the P&C reinsurance market seems to be past its peak and is now becoming a headwind for revenue growth in 2026. Despite that, I expect the company to maintain a relatively stable business profile ahead and deliver low-to-mid single-digit growth over the coming years, which doesn’t seem to be enough to push its valuation to higher multiples in the near future.

Given this background, I continue to see income as the most attractive feature of Munich RE’s investment case, as the company’s strong capitalization easily enables it to maintain its capital return strategy over the next few years.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Teleperformance: Leadership Reset And AI Transformation Reinforce A Deep Value Opportunity
data.description:
Teleperformance SE (TLPFF) (TLPFY) reported its full-year 2025 results on 27 February 2026. On the day the results were released, the stock reacted positively, and after nearly a year of negative performance (Fig. 1), the company finally appears to have stabilized.

For our new readers, Teleperformance is the global market leader in outsourced customer experience services. Here at the Lab, we take a contrarian stance on AI, supported by a series of analyses that underpin our equity investment: 1) AI Upside To Price In, and 2) partnerships with leading Agentic AI companies: Ema and Parloa. Additionally, the company's new “Future Forward” plan aims to accelerate transformation and cost savings. With a compelling valuation and an FCF yield above 20%, it was a clear buy. Our overweight rating was reiterated in the Q3 release, which, given the quarterly figures' limited release, we were positive on Teleperformance's confirmation of its 2025 outlook.

Fig. 1

Teleperformance Results and Our Positive View

The company closed the year with top-line sales of €10.2 billion (Fig. 2). Sales in 2025 were 0.7% lower than in the 2024 financial year. Teleperformance reported Q4 2025 organic growth of -0.6% yearly and a deceleration compared to the +1.4% achieved in Q3. As has been the pattern throughout 2025, Specialized Services remained under the most pressure (impacted by the non-renewal of a visa application management contract), but the slowdown in the Core segment was a bigger miss. From a quantitative standpoint, the recent positive reaction in Teleperformance’s share price does not appear to be supported by the company’s underlying fundamentals. On a positive note, Teleperformance achieved its 2025 target, and despite anticipating a slowdown in Q1, these details are overshadowed by the significant changes the company announced. Before that, EBITDA reached €2 billion, with strong resilience in language solutions and an EBITA margin of 14.6%, including currency effects. FCF generation exceeded our estimates, with record-high generation in H2. In total, FCF reached €901 million.

Source: Teleperformance 2025 FY ppt—Fig. 2

Why Are We Still Positive?

As already reported, there were strategic changes in executive leadership. Jorge Amar has been appointed CEO and will assume the role in mid-March 2026. Founder and current CEO Daniel Julien will step down from the executive team on the same date, together with Deputy CEO Thomas Mackenbrock, who had previously been considered a potential successor. Deputy CEO and Group CFO Olivier Rigaudy will also leave his position as he retires. Amar joins Teleperformance from McKinsey, where he led the firm’s Global Digital Customer Care practice for more than a decade and advised several of Teleperformance’s largest clients. Over the past year, we understood that he has already been working closely with the company’s senior management on several transformation initiatives. These changes are also combined with board-level new appointments. Here at the Lab, we believe Teleperformance needed a complete reset of its leadership team.

Teleperformance is advancing its “Future Forward” strategy through the launch of an internal AI-driven efficiency program expected to deliver more than €100 million in annualized cost savings (Fig. 3). For 2026, the company anticipates workforce adaptation and restructuring costs of approximately €70–90 million. Management expects roughly half of the targeted savings to materialize during 2026. Again, we have our contrarian view on AI, and we also believe Teleperformance can deliver savings with its AI Transformation.

Fig. 3

The incoming CEO will likely initiate a comprehensive strategic review of the company’s portfolio to reassess business exposures and potential divestments. This review will run alongside the Value Creation Office established in Q3 2025 and will cover both the Core and Specialized Services segments. This is supportive. Businesswise, we believe FX disruption will be limited this year. In a challenging operating environment, the company maintains a stable outlook for 2026, with sales slightly up, margins aligned with 2025, and solid FCF generation. Key to report is the uplift in dividend per share (Fig. 4). Teleperformance trades at a dividend yield of 7.5%.

Fig. 4

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Core Services slowed in Q4 as activity in onshore markets slowed. The Specialized Services segment remained in double-digit decline; however, adjusting for the visa contract, the decline was only -5.3% annually. We reported last time that "Teleperformance is navigating challenges," and, based on our previous analysis, we were accurate in assessing the company's finances. We still value Teleperformance at 2025 numbers; now we roll over our valuation. So, the company is guiding for +0-2% yearly organic sales (Fig. 5) but expects to start the year below this range. Here at the lab, we remain prudent for now, given the cautious Q1 outlook, but we need to report that the margin was more resilient than feared in 2025, with a notable H2 rebound. This was also true for FCF generation. And again, FCF generation in 2026 is supportive, with an outlook set between €800-850 million. So, we forecast top-line sales at the 2025 level (€10.2 billion). We see the 2026 margin impacted by deflationary dynamics, higher IT restructuring costs, and the implementation of new AI. Therefore, for prudency reasons, we are slightly lowering the EBITA margin by 20 basis points to 14.2%, deriving an EBITA of €1.44 billion. Considering the dividend payment, the company has €400/500 of deleverage optionality, which is significant given the current €4 billion net debt. We apply no change to our interest rate payment of €220 million, and we also confirmed a 30% corporate tax rate. Our net income reached €859 million, and assuming no share buyback and the current outstanding shares, our EPS reached €14.39 (slightly up from our previous estimate of €13.84).

This is a valuation play. Last time, we reported how "the company trades on a P/E and FCF yield of 4.3x and 25%, respectively. This is not a distressed company; Teleperformance generates operating cash flow, pays dividends, and has an ongoing buyback program." This is true. Even applying lower estimates, Teleperformance's P/E is at 4.16x, and its FCF yield is >22%. With no changes to our valuation methodology, we applied a blended P/E target of 10x (€143.9 per share) and an EV/EBITA of 5.5x (€65 per share), increasing our target price to €104.45 per share. The Teleperformance ADR traded in the US is TLPFY (OTC market), and the ADR ratio is equivalent to 0.5 ordinary Paris shares, so we derive an ADR target price of $60.48.

Fig. 5

Risks

We have long covered Teleperformance. So, our main downside risks are lower sales from new players and a change in FX. The company is exposed to regulatory risks. As a reminder, Teleperformance suffered significantly in 2003 due to strict regulations in the US call industry. Given a new CEO, the company might experience P&L volatility as a result of restructuring initiatives. We also report a higher short position in the shares. A higher short interest usually signals bearish sentiment among investors. This might lead to a volatile stock price. Therefore, we are not surprised to see that small earnings misses could prompt significant reductions in valuation.

Conclusion

Even applying lower estimates than Teleperformance's 2026 outlook and maintaining a fair valuation multiple, the company trades at a > 40% discount. The company communicated a strategic change for 2026, and the new CEO is a key positive catalyst for the stock going forward. We remain buyers.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Drop In Payrolls
data.description:
By David W. Berson, Ph.D.

A drop in nonfarm payrolls – survey volatility or real weakness? Nonfarm payrolls fell by 92,000 in February, compared with market expectations of a rise of around 50,000. Additionally, revisions to the prior two months totaled a drop of 69,000 (which brought December to a decline as well).

The U-3 unemployment rate edged up to 4.4 percent, while the broader U-6 rate slipped to 7.9 percent. Household employment fell by 185,000 (always more volatile than the nonfarm payroll figures given the smaller sample size).

All of this occurred before the run-up in energy prices brought about by the conflict with Iran.

What should we make of this surprisingly weak employment report? Does it represent the start of a weakening labor market? Is it simply volatility in a survey where the standard error (the 90 percent confidence interval) is more than 200,000? Clearly one month’s worth of data makes the answer to these questions uncertain, and we will have to wait for next month’s report to have a better idea. Other data do not corroborate the drop in payrolls – ADP, ISM, and NFIB surveys all were relatively positive with respect to the jobs. And recall that because of demographic and immigration factors, the trend rate of monthly payroll gains is in the 50,000-70,000 range – much lower than the historical average.

Will the Fed respond to this weak report by easing at the March FOMC meeting? Almost certainly not until there is less uncertainty about the underlying path of job growth. But if nonfarm payroll gains continue to be weak in the coming months, the odds of the Fed easing in April and/or June would rise. The Fed is likely to maintain its “watchful waiting” approach to monetary policy until the path of job creation is clearer. Complicating all of this is the spike in energy prices over the past week in response to the conflict in the Middle East. The sooner that conflict ends and energy prices retreat again, the smaller the impact on economic activity and inflation. But if the price hikes are sustained (or worsen), then, as in all negative supply shocks, we should see slower growth and higher inflation. Given the huge gains in U.S. oil and natural gas production in recent decades, the impact of such a supply shock is likely to be much less than in the supply shocks of the past – but it won’t be nothing.

Also released today was the January retail sales report – which showed a drop of 0.2 percent. But almost all of this came from a sharp decline in auto sales. The core measure of retail sales (excluding autos, gasoline, and building materials) rose by 0.3 percent, not a bad figure at all.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Bank Of America: Slipping Back Into The 'Buy' Zone
data.description:
Down around 12%, shares of Bank of America (BAC) are off to a rough start to 2026. Alongside conflict in the Middle East, we can add concerns surrounding private credit, the impact of artificial intelligence on jobs, and tariffs to the list of things that seem to be weighing on the Charlotte-based giant.

I first covered BAC in late 2024. Rating it "Buy," I liked BAC's valuation and the outlook for its interest margin, which had been relatively subdued due to the specifics of its balance sheet.

This pullback is an opportune time to revisit this investment case. Trading for just 12.5x trailing earnings, BAC once again looks cheap in the context of its growth prospects. I reiterate my previous "Buy" rating.

Interest Margin Expansion

BAC has taken a lot of flak in recent years. Among the charges are that it has been too conservative, particularly compared to high-flying peer JPMorgan (JPM), its closest comp. The numbers certainly seem to back that up. Whether we're talking about profitability, growth, or investment returns, JPM has outgunned BAC, and it’s not even close.

BAC's decision during the COVID years to deploy a slug of its non-maturing deposits into long-dated bonds has come to symbolize this criticism. Back in 2019, the bank had $215 billion in held-to-maturity investment securities on its balance sheet, equal to just over 10% of its total interest-earning assets. These figures had risen to $523 billion and 17%, respectively, as of last quarter.

Being as generous as I could, I did say that it may be prudent to adopt a "through-the-cycle" view of all this. Yes, the consensus is that BAC took on a bit too much duration risk in the early 2020s; however, these bonds could essentially act as a hedge for BAC during the easing cycle and beyond, supporting its interest margin and income. Furthermore, it should be noted just how strong BAC's deposit franchise is, particularly in its Consumer Banking unit, where the rate paid on its deposits was just 0.55% last quarter and 0.65% at the peak of the hiking cycle.

Anyway, this "hedge" is playing out. Despite a circa 135 basis points decline in the effective Fed funds rate, BAC's net interest margin has been rising nicely since my opening piece, as has its net interest income ("NII").

The good news: BAC still has a long repricing runway ahead of it. Per the latest earnings call, there'll be around $12-$15 billion in MBSs and residential mortgages rolling off the book each quarter this year.

During 2026, we expect roughly $12 billion to $15 billion in combined mortgage-backed securities and mortgage loans to roll off quarterly, and those will be replaced with new assets at 150 to 200 basis points higher in yield or they'll allow us to pay down expensive short-term debt.

Alastair Borthwick, Executive VP & CFO

As you can see below, this process won't be limited to 2026, either, with management guiding for over $60 billion in annual maturities over the 2027-2031 period.

Whether BAC replaces these low-yielding assets like-for-like, plows them into other assets, or uses them to pay down its more expensive forms of funding, the upshot will be much the same: a wider net interest margin and rising income. Specifically, management is targeting a 2.4% firm-wide net interest margin in the long run, around 32 basis points above its Q4 2025 level, and circa 5%-7% annualized NII growth.

Bottom Line Impact

BAC has an incredibly diverse business. Alongside the net interest income it generated from its loans, securities, and cash, the bank also booked around $10.18 billion in fee income last quarter, up nearly 7% year-on-year.

Why, then, have I focused so narrowly on its interest margin expansion? Two reasons, really. First, margin-driven NII growth doesn't really attract any incremental expense growth, so this revenue largely flows right on down to pre-tax income. That's precisely what BAC has seen recently, with last quarter's revenue growth outpacing cost growth by around 330 basis points. As a result, pre-tax pre-provision profit rose nearly 13% year-on-year. Management expects another 200 basis points of operating leverage in 2026.

Second, unlike a growing loan book, BAC doesn't need to hold more capital against a higher net interest margin. This makes it highly profitable growth. Last year, BAC earned $29.1 billion in net income against roughly $204 billion in average tangible common equity, for a return of 14.2%. This was an improvement of nearly 130 basis points compared to 2024.

These earnings will largely be headed toward shareholders' pockets. Indeed, given its size, resultant growth prospects, solid profitability, and capital cushion, BAC has to spend most of its profits on shareholder remuneration to avoid accumulating surplus capital. It did $8.1 billion in dividends last year and $21.4 billion in buybacks. Per above, the latter resulted in a 5.2% decline in BAC's share count on a period-end basis, boosting its share-level bottom line beyond its net income growth.

Shares Look Like A Bargain

BAC trades for $47.94 as of this writing, equal to 12.5x last year's earnings per share. This is a little below its ten-year average, as shown below.

I find that interesting given the outlook for BAC's profits. The short version: driven by the operating leverage outlined in the last section, BAC's bottom line is going to swell considerably in the coming years. Consensus has 2028 net income exceeding $36 billion, around 25% higher than last year's print.

More profits will mean more cash for buybacks. This will be a tailwind for earnings per share, which is expected to increase to over $5.50 in the next few years, equating to a 13% annualized rate of growth. Coupled with BAC's 2.3% dividend yield, this gets today's buyer to a circa 15% annualized return.

Being a bank, there are plenty of risks to consider here. Per below, BAC's net loan losses have actually been heading in the right direction, but this could turn if the economy heads south, forcing BAC to boost its provisions. This would eat into its profits.

Above the provisioning line, BAC's revenue could face a few headwinds. For example, its fee income is sensitive to many things, including capital market levels. While fees rose 7% last quarter, over 75% of this growth came from asset management fees within BAC's Wealth segment. At the risk of stating the obvious, this business generates a lot of asset-based revenue. Accordingly, it has been a major beneficiary of elevated stock markets, which have delivered three straight years of double-digit returns. A pullback would weigh on this unit's results. Finally, a sharp fall in interest rates would naturally derail the whole fixed-asset repricing thesis, weighing on BAC's net interest revenue.

These are all risks that prospective investors will need to mull over. However, with BAC offering a mid-teens annualized return from its business and with shares currently trading below their long-run trailing P/E ratio, I think these risks are worth taking on. I rate shares "Buy."

data.title: Petrobras Q4 Earnings: Dividend Yield Higher Than P/E
data.description:
Petróleo Brasileiro: ~7% FWD Yield Meets 5.5x FWD P/E

I last analyzed Petrobras (NYSE:PBR) stock on Jan 29 with an article titled “Venezuela Catalysts Can Benefit Suncor Energy More Than Petrobras.” It focused on the developments in Venezuela and rated PBR as a hold. Since then, a few new developments have emerged in terms of macroeconomics (in particular the Iran conflict and its impact on global oil prices) and PBR’s specific financials, announced in the last Q4 2025 earnings report (ER), as shown in the snapshot below.

With heightened geopolitical risks, I feel it’s especially timely to focus on the return vector that involves the least degree of uncertainty. The vector with the least ambiguity is dividend in my book. As such, this article will focus on the changes in PBR’s dividends and, more importantly, my forward view for future payouts. In the end, my assessment is that the lucrative dividend, especially when combined with low P/E, provides such a thick cushion and makes the stock a solid hold amid global oil uncertainties. This high-yield and low-P/E combination is so extreme that it appears absurd to me. As detailed next, the stock's FWD dividend hovers around 7%, which is higher than its FWD P/E of 5.5x by a good gap (that is, if you only consider the numerical values without the percentage sign).

PBR Stock: Latest Dividend Announcement

In tandem with the ER, the PBR board just approved the latest dividend payout. The approval brought the full-year payout to R$41.2 (with more details quoted below). At the current FX rate, this translates into about $7.77 billion US dollars.

TipRanks News (Mar 6, 2026): On March 5, 2026, Petrobras’ board approved a proposal to submit to the April 16, 2026, Annual General Meeting a distribution of R$ 8.1 billion in shareholder remuneration for the fourth quarter of 2025, bringing total 2025 payouts to R$ 41.2 billion after Selic-rate adjustments and deductions. The remuneration aligns with policy to distribute 45% of free cash flow, with payments in May and June 2026.

As such, even at the stock’s current market capitalization of around $113 billion, which is around the 52-week high, the dividend yield is about 6.9%. With the average market capitalization of $82.3 billion in the past year, the annualized yield is close to 9.5%.

PBR Stock: Dividend Outlook

Looking ahead, the dividend payouts are well covered and have good potential to increase further in my model. With the Q4 ER, total capex totaled US$18.6 billion on a TTM basis, as shown in the cash flow statement below. This is a sizable increase of more than 44% over the 2024 level. Judging by the updates provided in the ER, the company kept making progress on the FPSO P-78 construction and explorations of new assets, particularly in the Equatorial Margin.

Despite the surges in capex, free cash flow (FCF) remains robust and the dividends well covered. As shown in the statement above, cash from operations totaled more than $36.3 billion, resulting in an FCF of around $17.6 billion as of TTM (or $2.72 per ADR). The dividend budget of $7.77 billion thus represents about 44% of the total FCF, well aligned with the company’s goal of distributing 45% of its FCF as dividends, as aforementioned.

Looking ahead, I expect the surges in capital investments to be more tempered and the past capex investments to start contributing. The divestitures of non-core assets could also help release some capex pressure. For example, in the past quarter, PBR completed the auction of the uncontracted areas in Mero and Atapu. All told, my estimates point to an FCF of around $3.2 per ADR. With an FCF payout ratio of 45%, this estimate further translates into a dividend of $1.44 per ADR and a dividend yield of more than 8.3% at the share price as of this writing.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

In terms of downside risks, PBR entails all the risks generic to other oil companies, such as sensitivity to oil prices and geopolitical conflicts. Here, I will focus on a few risks that are more unique to PBR and the analysis presented above. Unlike the US oil stocks most energy investors are familiar with (the likes of ExxonMobil or Chevron), Petrobras is a state-controlled entity. The Brazilian government is the majority shareholder and possesses golden shares that allow it to directly influence company leadership and strategy. This control may not always align with shareholders’ interests, and the company has suffered frequent leadership turnover in recent years.

Also, in all my above analysis, I used the current BRL (Brazilian Real) to USD exchange rate of 1 BRL to 0.19 USD, as shown in the chart below. However, as you can also see from the chart, the BRL-USD exchange rates have fluctuated considerably in recent years. Between the two years of 2023 and 2025, the FX rate changes from a peak of about 0.21 to a trough of 0.16, a change of more than 30%, quite extreme for a major currency.

There are a few fundamental reasons behind such large fluctuations. As a major exporter of iron ore, oil, and agricultural products, the BRL has a strong flavor of a commodity currency in my mind. Fluctuations in global demand for these resources directly impact the BRL. For example, the decline in the chart during late 2024 often correlates with periods of cooling global industrial demand or shifts in energy prices. In the meantime, Brazil has been faced with elevated inflation in recent years (and is still facing the issue in my view). To fight inflation, the Brazil central bank has maintained relatively high-interest rates. A higher interest rate tends to encourage foreign carry trade investors. However, the relative attractiveness of trade is dynamic and depends on how central banks in other countries (i.e., the US Federal Reserve) set their interest rates. As these dynamics shift, the BRL-USD FX rate could continue experiencing high volatilities.

To close, this PBR revisit is motivated by the ongoing geopolitical conflicts, its Q4 ER, and also the latest dividend announcements. These changes have made me feel it is especially timely for investors to focus on dividends, the return vector that involves the least degree of uncertainty. Admittedly, FX rate change still represents a sizable uncertainty (as much as 30% in recent years). However, even with a 30% margin of error, I feel the core thesis should still stand, which is that the combination of high yield and low P/E makes the stock a solid hold despite all the downsides.

data.title: IMOM: Better Tactical ETF Than Long-Term Holding
data.description:
IMOM Strategy

Alpha Architect Intl Quantitative Momentum ETF (IMOM) is an actively managed fund launched on 12/22/2015, with an objective of long-term capital appreciation. IMOM has a portfolio of 50 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.44%, and an expense ratio of 0.38%. Distributions are paid annually. The fund is managed by Dr. Wesley Gray and Dr. Jack Vogel, authors of the book Quantitative Momentum (Wiley).

As described in the prospectus by Alpha Architect, the methodology starts from the largest 1,500 stocks by market capitalization listed in countries included in the MSCI EAFE Index, then reduces the investing universe to the 500 with the highest total returns over the past 12 months, excluding the most recent month. A quantitative, rules-based process identifies highly liquid stocks with the most consistent positive returns during the trailing 12 months. Finally, the top 50 to 200 momentum stocks are included as constituents.

The portfolio turnover is high: 411% in the most recent fiscal year. This article will use as a benchmark the MSCI EAFE Index, represented by iShares MSCI EAFE ETF (EFA).

Portfolio

IMOM has 64% of asset value in large- and mega-cap companies and 36% in mid-caps. The portfolio has a focus on Japan (34.9%) and notable exposure in Germany (17.6%). No other country weighs more than 10%. Compared to the benchmark, IMOM mostly overweights Japan, Germany, and Israel, while it greatly downplays the U.K., France, and Switzerland.

The fund is concentrated in three sectors: industrials (32%), financials (21.2%), and materials (20.1%). Other sectors are below 6%. The sector breakdown is not typical of the strategy; it may change with sector relative momentum.

The portfolio is diversified, with low company-specific risk. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 22.1% of asset value, and the largest position weighs 2.5%. Nonetheless, IMOM is more concentrated than EFA, where the top 10 names weigh only 13.6%.

Fundamentals

IMOM is classified by Morningstar in the “Foreign Large Blend” ETF category, but it shows a strong inclination to growth. Indeed, it has significantly higher aggregate growth rates compared to EFA (especially cash flow growth), as reported in the next table. Additionally, its valuation ratios are close to the benchmark, unlike most growth funds. In summary, IMOM has solid growth characteristics without sacrificing value.

IMOM has the narrowest portfolio and the highest turnover. It is the best performer over the past year but comes in last position for total return and Sharpe ratio since November 2017. Additionally, it has the highest risk metrics. In this time frame, IDMO leads the pack by a wide margin.

Takeaway

Alpha Architect Intl Quantitative Momentum ETF has a high-conviction portfolio of 50 stocks with high and steady returns over the past 12 months. IMOM has strong growth characteristics without sacrificing value. Company-specific risk is low, but the portfolio is heavily weighted in Japan and industrials. IMOM has underperformed the benchmark EFA and momentum ETF peers in the long term but has outperformed them over the past year. Due to its excellent performance in short periods of time and high volatility, IMOM is best suited for tactical allocation. Based on historical performance and risk metrics, IDMO looks like a better alternative for long-term international exposure in the momentum factor.
